text: The surgeon operates with the camera positioned in the left upper quadrant and the right hand in the right upper quadrant near the costal margins. Two left-sided trocars are used, allowing access to the sigmoid fistula portion of the procedure.

The surgeon removes the omentum from the area of disease, revealing the terminal ileum and cecum adhered to the right dome of the bladder and the right pelvic sidewall. The surgeon inspects for interloop fistulas and observes adherence to the sigmoid colon, aiming to preserve the sigmoid colon during dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the sigmoid colon from the ileocolic valve, ensuring no injury to surrounding tissues. The sigmoid appears free, but further inspection reveals it is adhered to the ileocolic valve.

The surgeon attempts to separate the distal sigmoid colon, which is plastered to the lateral aspect of the ileum, demonstrating how the loop extends into the pelvis and returns. The connection is broad-based, mesentery to mesentery, rather than bowel wall to bowel wall. During separation, the surgeon enters a granulation-filled cavity, leading to an unavoidable injury, prompting the resection of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon examines the ileocolic valve and the surrounding diseased tissue, preparing to perform a resection and anastomosis of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection and focused diathermy to assess the extent of the disease in the sigmoid colon, using the current port configuration for the resection. The robotic system is undocked and redocked for the subsequent ileocolic resection.

The surgeon completes the necessary mobilization of the sigmoid colon, revealing the diseased area. A sponge is placed to manage oozing from the inflammatory fistula. The surgeon marks the proximal sigmoid margin near the fistula and prepares to take the mesentery.

The surgeon completes the division of the sigmoid colon proximally and distally, placing the resected specimen aside. The sigmoid anastomosis is performed using an inner layer of VLOC sutures and an outer layer of PDS sutures.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis despite the challenging angle, ensuring the connection is secure. A leak test is then performed to verify the integrity of the anastomosis.

The surgeon observes a healthy, non-bleeding, widely patent anastomosis. The robotic system is redocked in the standard configuration for a right colectomy or ileocolic resection, preparing to address the remaining diseased tissue.

The surgeon utilizes five ports and two twelve millimeter trocars for stapling, with one suprapubic port used for extraction. The bowel is run proximally, revealing a transition to more normal bowel tissue. No additional disease is observed, indicating the extent of the ileocolic resection.

The surgeon continues a lateral to medial dissection plane, opting not to perform high ligations of the mesentery for this case of Crohn's disease. The dissection progresses with attention to the mesentery, considering the potential benefits of complete mesocolic excision (CME) when feasible.
The surgeon selects the colon margin for resection, avoiding high ligation due to the thickness of the mesentery near the root of the superior mesenteric vessels.

The surgeon marks the small bowel margin and creates a window, then dissects the mesentery from the proximal to distal margin. The dissection stays close to the bowel wall, wrapping around the branches of the ileocolic pedicle towards the colon mesentery, ensuring a good amount of mesentery is available for suturing, clipping, or stapling if needed.

The surgeon has divided the colon and is now proceeding to divide the small bowel mesentery. The robotic approach is utilized for ease of movement and ergonomic benefits during the minimally invasive procedure. The final pathology confirmed Crohn's disease with a fistula to the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon completes the intracorporeal anastomosis by finishing the inner layer closure of the common channel. Indocyanine green (ICG) is checked to ensure satisfactory perfusion. The surgeon adjusts the suture holding the valve and reinforces the closure with a second layer.
The surgeon covers the distal anastomosis with healthy omentum and extracts both specimens through the Pfannenstiel incision, which corresponds to one of the twelve millimeter trocar sites.
